license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Windows Defender protection history deleted
entries.summary: A history item was removed from the Windows Defender protection history.
entries.details: Generated when an item is removed from Windows Defender's protection history. Protection history records past detections, quarantine actions, and remediation events. Deletion of history items — particularly around the timeframe of a compromise — can obscure the record of what was detected and what actions were taken. Correlate with EID 1116 and EID 1117 entries in the same timeframe to determine whether history deletion follows a detection event, which is a strong anti-forensic indicator.

investigationPivots: Correlate the deletion timestamp with nearby EID 1116/1117 events — deletion shortly after a detection is a strong cleanup indicator
Identify what process or user account triggered the history deletion via Security EID 4688/4624 correlation
Consider retrieving Defender history from alternate sources (MDE portal, SIEM) if local history was deleted
commonFalsePositives: Users or IT manually clearing Defender history via the Windows Security app
Automated cleanup policies removing old history entries

entries.title: Suspicious behavior detected
entries.summary: Windows Defender detected a potentially suspicious behavior.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender's behavioral monitoring detects a suspicious activity pattern. Unlike EID 1116 (signature-based detection), EID 1015 fires on behavioral heuristics — such as a process performing operations characteristic of credential dumping, code injection, or persistence installation — even if no known malware signature matches. Key fields: Process Name, the behavior type or category, and the affected resource. Behavioral detections that do not result in a subsequent EID 1116/1117 (no signature match) may represent novel or fileless threats.

investigationPivots: Process Name is the primary pivot — behavioral detections in LOLBins (powershell.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe) or Office applications are high priority
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to review the full process ancestry and command line
A 1015 with no subsequent 1117 may mean Defender flagged but did not remediate — the activity may still be active
commonFalsePositives: Security testing and red team tools triggering behavioral heuristics
Legitimate software performing unusual but benign operations (e.g., custom backup tools, scripting automation)

entries.title: Malware or unwanted software detected
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender detects malware or potentially unwanted software. Key fields: Threat Name, Category, Severity Level, Detection Source (real-time, on-access, scheduled scan, etc.), Process Name (the process associated with the detection), and Path (the file or object flagged). Correlate Threat Name against the detection timeline to identify what the attacker was running when Defender fired. Process Name is especially valuable — detections attributed to LOLBins (e.g., powershell.exe, mshta.exe, wscript.exe) are higher priority than detections in temp directories.

investigationPivots: Pivot on Process Name to identify what was executing the detected payload
Correlate Path with file creation events to determine how the file arrived on the system
Detection Source of 'Real-Time Protection' means the threat was caught during active execution; 'Scheduled Scan' may indicate an older dormant file
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 to find related process activity
commonFalsePositives: Security research tools or red team utilities flagged as PUA/PUP
Packed or obfuscated legitimate software triggering heuristic detections

entries.title: Antivirus took action to protect machine from malware
entries.summary: Windows Defender took a remediation action on a detected threat.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender performs a remediation action following a detection (EID 1116). Key fields: Threat Name, Action (Quarantine, Remove, Allow, Block, Clean), Action Status (success or failure), and ThreatID. Action Status of failure means the threat was detected but not successfully remediated — the file or process may still be active. Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to link detection and response. An action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' may indicate the threat was excluded or the user overrode the detection.

investigationPivots: Action Status failure means the file or process may still be present — escalate immediately
Action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' warrants investigation into whether an exclusion was in place
Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to get the full detection context including file path and process
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software quarantined due to false positive detection
Security tooling excluded from scanning triggering no-action outcomes

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in block mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule blocked a potentially malicious operation.
entries.details: Generated when an Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rule fires in block mode and prevents an operation. Key fields: the rule GUID (identifies which ASR rule triggered), Process Name (the process that attempted the blocked operation), and Path (the target file or object). Each ASR rule GUID maps to a specific rule such as 'Block Office applications from creating executable content' or 'Block credential stealing from Windows LSASS'. An ASR block during an incident is a high-confidence signal that an attacker technique was attempted and stopped.

investigationPivots: Map the rule GUID to a specific ASR rule using Microsoft's ASR rule reference to understand what technique was blocked
Process Name reveals what attempted the blocked operation — flag Office apps, scripting engines, or LOLBins
Correlate timestamp and process with Security EID 4688 to build the full execution chain
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Office macros or scripts blocked by overly broad ASR rules
Developer tools triggering code-execution ASR rules

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in audit mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule would have blocked an operation but is in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when an ASR rule fires in audit mode — the operation was allowed to proceed but would have been blocked in enforcement mode. Key fields are identical to EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, and Path. Audit mode events are valuable during incident investigations to identify attacker techniques that were attempted but not blocked, particularly in environments where ASR rules have not yet been moved to enforcement. Treat audit events from known-bad processes with the same urgency as block events.

prerequisites.description: Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules must be enabled and configured in audit mode via Microsoft Defender, Intune, or Group Policy.

investigationPivots: Audit mode means the operation succeeded — treat as executed attacker activity, not just an attempt
Same pivot fields as EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, Path
Use audit events to identify which ASR rules should be promoted to block mode
commonFalsePositives: Same as EID 1121 — audit mode means no blocking occurred, so false positive impact is limited to alert noise

entries.title: Security intelligence update succeeded
entries.summary: Windows Defender security intelligence (signatures) updated successfully.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender successfully updates its security intelligence (antimalware signatures). Key fields: the new signature version and the update source. Monitoring the cadence of EID 2001 events is useful for establishing a baseline — a host that suddenly stops receiving updates (no 2001 events for an extended period) may be isolated from update sources, have update mechanisms disabled, or be experiencing network-level blocking of update traffic consistent with a compromised host trying to avoid signature-based detection of its malware.

investigationPivots: Absence of EID 2001 events over an extended period may indicate update mechanisms have been disabled or blocked
Correlate last successful update timestamp with the timing of a compromise to determine whether signatures were outdated when the attack occurred
Hosts with significantly outdated signature versions are higher-risk targets
commonFalsePositives: Air-gapped or isolated hosts that rely on manual signature updates
Hosts on metered connections with update scheduling configured to minimize bandwidth

entries.title: Security intelligence update failed
entries.summary: Windows Defender failed to update its security intelligence.
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender security intelligence update fails. Key fields: error code and the update source that was attempted. Repeated EID 2002 failures on an otherwise internet-connected host may indicate that the host cannot reach Microsoft update servers — potentially because an attacker has modified the hosts file, DNS, firewall rules, or proxy settings to block updates and prevent new signatures from detecting their tools. Correlate with network log sources to determine whether update traffic is being blocked at the host or network level.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Persistent update failures on an internet-connected host may indicate blocked update traffic — investigate hosts file, DNS, and firewall rules
Correlate with EID 5007 to check whether proxy or update server configuration was recently changed
An update failure immediately preceding a malware execution event indicates the host may have been left intentionally unprotected
commonFalsePositives: Transient network connectivity issues causing temporary update failures
Proxy or firewall misconfiguration blocking Windows Update traffic

entries.title: Real-time protection is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender real-time protection was turned off.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender real-time protection is disabled. This is a high-priority alert during an incident — disabling real-time protection is a common attacker technique to prevent detection of subsequent payloads. Correlate the timestamp with process creation and logon events to identify what account or process disabled protection. Legitimate causes include Group Policy-managed AV exclusions, third-party AV coexistence, and IT administrative actions; these should be validated against change management records.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what process or account made the change
Check whether the change corresponds to a known Group Policy or administrative action
Real-time protection disabled immediately before a malware detection or payload execution is a strong compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators temporarily disabling protection for maintenance or software installation
Third-party AV products disabling Defender as part of coexistence management
Group Policy enforcing a disabled state on managed endpoints

entries.title: Real-time protection configuration changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender real-time protection setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when a real-time protection-specific configuration setting is changed. While EID 5007 captures all configuration changes, EID 5004 specifically flags modifications to real-time protection settings such as monitoring scope, behavior monitoring, network protection, or on-access scanning. These changes are higher priority than generic configuration changes because they directly affect the ability to detect active threats. Key field: the specific real-time protection setting that was changed and its new value.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: The changed setting and its new value are the key pivot — changes reducing scan coverage or disabling behavioral monitoring are high priority
Correlate with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify the process and account that made the change
EID 5004 paired with EID 5001 in close succession confirms real-time protection was both reconfigured and disabled
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy or MDM applying real-time protection policy changes
Third-party AV management tools modifying Defender real-time settings for coexistence

entries.title: Antimalware platform configuration changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender configuration setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when any Windows Defender configuration setting changes. Key field: the old and new configuration values, recorded in the format 'HKLM\...\value = old value -> new value'. Configuration changes of particular interest include additions to ExclusionPath, ExclusionProcess, or ExclusionExtension (attackers add exclusions to prevent detection of their tools), and changes to DisableRealtimeMonitoring, DisableBehaviorMonitoring, or DisableIOAVProtection. Exclusion additions should be correlated against known software deployments.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Changes to ExclusionPath or ExclusionProcess are the highest-priority — they may be hiding attacker tooling
Correlate the changed value with the file or directory path to determine what is being excluded
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what made the configuration change
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installers adding Defender exclusions during setup
Group Policy or MDM pushing exclusion policies to managed endpoints

entries.title: Antimalware engine failure
entries.summary: The Windows Defender antimalware engine encountered an error and failed.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Defender antimalware engine encounters a fatal error and fails. While engine failures can be caused by resource constraints or software bugs, they can also result from deliberate interference — an attacker crashing or corrupting the Defender engine to prevent detection of subsequent activity. Key field: the error code. Correlate the engine failure timestamp with nearby process creation events, network connections, or file writes to identify whether the failure coincides with attacker activity.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate the engine failure timestamp with Security EID 4688 to identify process activity occurring immediately before or after the failure
Engine failures that cannot be explained by known software issues or resource constraints warrant investigation into potential tampering
Check whether the engine failure was followed by a recovery (EID 5001 state change) or left the system unprotected
commonFalsePositives: Engine crashes due to bugs in specific Defender versions — check for pending updates
Resource exhaustion on underpowered systems causing engine failures

entries.title: Scanning for malware and unwanted software is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender malware and PUP scanning was disabled.
entries.details: Generated when scanning for malware and potentially unwanted programs (PUP) is disabled. This is distinct from EID 5001 (real-time protection disabled) — scanning can be selectively disabled while real-time protection remains nominally enabled. Disabling malware scanning creates a window during which new files can be written to disk and executed without signature-based detection. Treat this event with the same urgency as EID 5001 during an active incident.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what process/account made the change
Files written to disk after scanning was disabled should be treated as potentially unscanned and uninspected
Correlate with EID 5007 to identify the specific registry key or configuration value changed
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy-managed configurations that selectively disable certain scanning features
Temporary administrative disablement for performance testing or software compatibility troubleshooting

entries.title: Scanning for viruses is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender virus scanning was disabled.
entries.details: Generated when scanning specifically for viruses is disabled. Related to but distinct from EID 5010 (malware/PUP scanning disabled) — Windows Defender distinguishes between virus scanning and the broader malware/PUP scanning category. Disabling virus scanning leaves the system without signature-based detection for executable threats. Correlate with EID 5007 to identify the configuration change, and with Security EID 4688 to identify what process initiated the change.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify the process that disabled virus scanning
Pair with EID 5010 — if both fire around the same time, most active threat detection has been disabled
Check whether any files were written or processes executed in the window between EID 5012 and scanning being re-enabled
commonFalsePositives: Managed configurations that disable specific scan types on servers to reduce I/O overhead
Coexistence configurations where a third-party AV manages virus scanning
